findings.title: Cross-Domain Misconfiguration (CORS)
findings.description: The web server permits cross-domain read requests from arbitrary third party domains using the wildcard `*` in the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. This allows unauthenticated APIs to be accessed from any origin.
findings.remediation: Configure the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` HTTP header to a more restrictive set of trusted domains, or remove all CORS headers entirely to enforce Same Origin Policy. Ensure sensitive data is not available in an unauthenticated manner.

metadata.impact: Attackers can create malicious web pages that read data from unauthenticated APIs on this domain, potentially exposing sensitive information that relies on other security controls (like IP whitelisting).
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, verification_status, false_positive_indicators; High overall confidence

findings.title: CORS Wildcard Configuration
findings.description: The application allows cross-origin requests from any origin, which can enable cross-origin attacks including CSRF and data exfiltration.

findings.title: Exposed SSH Service
findings.description: SSH service is accessible on port 22 with OpenSSH 9.6p1.
findings.evidence: `<port protocol="tcp" portid="22">` with `<state state="open" reason="syn-ack" reason_ttl="64"/>`
findings.remediation: Ensure SSH uses key-based authentication, disable root login, use non-standard port, and implement fail2ban or similar intrusion prevention.

metadata.impact: SSH provides remote access capability. If misconfigured or using weak authentication, could allow unauthorized access.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: CORS Wildcard Configuration
findings.description: The server responds with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * header, allowing any origin to make cross-origin requests to this application.
findings.remediation: Restrict Access-Control-Allow-Origin to specific trusted domains instead of using wildcard (*). Implement proper CORS validation based on origin.

metadata.impact: Enables cross-origin resource sharing from any domain, potentially allowing malicious sites to interact with the application and access sensitive data if the application exposes authenticated endpoints.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Public Cache Control
findings.description: The server sets Cache-Control to public, allowing any cache (including shared caches) to store the response.
findings.remediation: Use Cache-Control: private, no-store, no-cache for pages containing sensitive or user-specific information.

metadata.impact: Sensitive data in responses could be cached by intermediate proxies or browsers, potentially exposing information to unauthorized users.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Permissive CORS Configuration
findings.description: The server responds with a wildcard CORS header allowing any origin to make cross-origin requests to this application.
findings.remediation: Restrict Access-Control-Allow-Origin to specific trusted domains instead of using wildcard (*). Implement proper CORS validation based on the request origin.

metadata.impact: Could enable cross-origin data exfiltration or CSRF attacks if combined with other vulnerabilities. May allow malicious sites to read sensitive responses.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Exposed Backup and Archive Files
findings.description: The web server is exposing numerous backup files, database dumps, and archive files that should not be publicly accessible. Files include .sql (database dumps), .tar.gz, .zip, .war, .jks (Java key stores), .pem (private keys), and .cer (certificates).
findings.evidence: `+ /site.tar.gz: Potentially interesting backup/cert file found.` and 100+ similar entries including `+ /192.168.1.49.sql: Potentially interesting backup/cert file found.`
findings.remediation: 1) Remove all backup files from web-accessible directories. 2) Configure web server to deny access to backup file extensions (.sql, .tar, .gz, .zip, .war, .jks, .pem, .cer, .egg, .alz, .tgz, .bz2, .lzma). 3) Store backups on separate, non-web-accessible storage. 4) Implement proper access controls and authentication for sensitive files.

metadata.impact: Attackers can download sensitive data including source code, database contents, credentials, private keys, and configuration files. This could lead to complete system compromise, data exfiltration, and credential theft.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, context_relevance; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Overly Permissive CORS Configuration
findings.description: The server is configured with a wildcard CORS policy that allows any origin to access resources from this domain. This bypasses browser same-origin policy protections.
findings.evidence:
Evidence from nikto:
`+ /: Retrieved access-control-allow-origin header: *.`

---

Evidence from headers:
`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`
findings.remediation: 1) Replace wildcard (*) with specific trusted origins. 2) Implement proper CORS validation on the server side. 3) Use `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` with specific domains or implement CORS preflight validation. 4) Consider using `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: false` if cross-origin requests don't require credentials.

metadata.impact: Malicious websites can make authenticated requests to this API on behalf of users, potentially leading to CSRF-like attacks, data exfiltration, or unauthorized actions if the application uses cookies or other authentication mechanisms.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: robots.txt Information Disclosure
findings.description: The robots.txt file reveals the existence of an /ftp/ directory that returns HTTP 200, indicating it's accessible. This directory may contain sensitive files or information.
findings.evidence: `+ /robots.txt: Entry '/ftp/' is returned a non-forbidden or redirect HTTP code (200).`
findings.remediation: 1) Review and restrict access to the /ftp/ directory. 2) Remove or restrict sensitive entries in robots.txt. 3) Implement proper access controls on discovered directories. 4) Consider using robots.txt only for legitimate SEO purposes, not for hiding sensitive content.

metadata.impact: Attackers can discover hidden or sensitive directories that should not be publicly known. The /ftp/ directory may contain files with sensitive information, credentials, or system data.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, context_relevance, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Information Disclosure via Custom Header
findings.description: The server returns a custom header 'x-recruiting' that reveals internal routing information (/#/jobs). This provides reconnaissance information about the application structure.

metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: jQuery End-of-Life (No Security Updates)
findings.description: jQuery version 2.2.4 is part of the 1.x and 2.x series which has reached End-of-Life status and no longer receives security patches or updates.
findings.evidence: `"summary":"jQuery 1.x and 2.x are End-of-Life and no longer receiving security updates","severity":"low","identifiers":{"summary":"jQuery 1.x and 2.x are End-of-Life and no longer receiving security updates","retid":"73","issue":"162"},"cwe":["CWE-1104"]`
findings.remediation: Upgrade to jQuery 3.x or later (preferably 3.5.0+) which is actively maintained and receives security updates.

metadata.impact: Using EOL software means known vulnerabilities will not be patched, increasing long-term security risk.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: evidence_quality, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: CVE-2015-9251 - Cross-Site Scripting via CORS Request
findings.description: jQuery 2.2.4 is vulnerable to XSS attacks through third-party CORS requests. The vulnerability allows malicious scripts to execute when handling cross-origin requests.
findings.remediation: Upgrade jQuery to version 3.0.0-beta1 or higher. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers as defense-in-depth.

metadata.impact: Attackers can inject and execute malicious JavaScript code in the context of the victim's browser, potentially stealing session tokens, credentials, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: context_relevance, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: CVE-2019-11358 - Object Prototype Pollution
findings.description: jQuery 2.2.4 is vulnerable to Object.prototype pollution through jQuery.extend(true, {}, ...) which can modify the Object prototype chain.
findings.evidence: `"summary":"jQuery before 3.4.0, as used in Drupal, Backdrop CMS, and other products, mishandles jQuery.extend(true, {}, ...) because of Object.prototype pollution","CVE":["CVE-2019-11358"],"PR":"4333","githubID":"GHSA-6c3j-c64m-qhgq"},"cwe":["CWE-1321","CWE-79"]`
findings.remediation: Upgrade jQuery to version 3.4.0 or higher. Sanitize all user input before passing to jQuery.extend().

metadata.impact: Attackers can inject properties into Object.prototype, potentially leading to denial of service, privilege escalation, or remote code execution depending on how the application uses affected objects.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: evidence_quality, context_relevance, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

No security findings in output
